Whereas the power-law spectral indices of M82 and NGC 253 are 2.2--2.3 extending to TeV energies, the spectra of the LMC and SMC steepen above 2 GeV lat_2010_smc,lat_2010_lmc .

sources.summary: The paper presents M82 as a prototypical starburst in AKARI/IRAS comparisons and later uses redshifted M82 template SEDs to assess whether AKARI NEP depths would detect submillimeter galaxies.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:m82 compares AKARI diffuse maps and IRAS images of the prototypical starburst M82, plus its companion M81.

To demonstrate this, we return finally to the first local galaxy discussed in this paper, M82. Figure fig:nep shows redshifted M82 template SEDs normalised to the 500 m confusion limit compared to the AKARI NEP-Deep mid-infrared depths. The optical stellar populations will obviously not be representative of the high-redshift population but the mid-infrared fluxes should be realistic. The AKARI depths are clearly easily deep enough to detect the the 500 m population with this SED.

sources.summary: The paper uses M 82 as a local starburst SED template, finding comparable mid-infrared PAH features to the z 1 composite but less far-infrared emission and warmer dust.

quotes.quote: In the top panel of Fig. sb_local_temp , we compare our z 1 composite SF SED to the SED of local starburst M 82. Though M 82 has a lower L_ IR ( 4 10^ 10 L _ ) than our z 1 SED, it is worthwhile to compare the two SEDs, since our mid-IR classification is based on the M 82 template. We normalize M 82 to our composite SED at 7.0 m. Our z 1 composite SED almost exactly reproduces the shape and strength of the PAH features of M 82, affirming the reliability of comparing the mid-IR PAH features of this local galaxy with the mid-IR spectra of high redshift sources. However, M 82 has less far-IR emission, and it is weighted towards warmer dust than our z 1 SF composite, indicating that any mid-IR similarities do not carry over into the far- IR.

sources.summary: The paper describes M 82 molecular gas morphology, prior H2CO detections, and new high-confidence 4.8 and 14.5 GHz absorption measurements not reproduced by one LVG prediction.

quotes.quote: M 82: The morphology of M 82's molecular ISM is characterized by a double-lobed structure Mao2000 . imaged the CO J= 6-5 emission from this galaxy, finding emission extended over a disk-like structure 40^ 15^ in extent. H _2 CO was previously detected in absorption in the 1_ 10 1_ 11 line by Graham1978 , in emission in the 2_ 11 2_ 12 and 3_ 03 2_ 02 (218 GHz) lines by Baan1990 , and in emission in the 2_ 02 1_ 01 (146 GHz), 3_ 03 2_ 02 and 3_ 22 2_ 21 / 3_ 21 2_ 20 (218 GHz) lines by Muehle2007 . We detect both the 4.8 and 14.5 GHz lines in absorption with high confidence (Figure fig:NGC2146M82M82SWNGC3079FormSpec ). Investigation of our LVG model predictions of the relative intensities of the 4.8 and 14.5 GHz emission (see LVG ) cannot reproduce the 14.5 GHz emission observed by Baan1990 .

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as a starburst-galaxy SED-fitting benchmark, applying the same photometry as previous work and comparing SATMC best-fit parameters to SK07 models.

quotes.quote: We begin with the well known galaxies Arp 220 and M82 and the starburst SED library of (hereafter SK07) which has already been shown to provide reasonable fits to Arp 220 and M82. The SED library consists of over 7000 templates with emission from a starburst parametrized by its total luminosity L^ tot , nuclear radius R , visual extinction A_V , ratio of luminosity from OB stars to the total luminosity and hot spot dust density n . The observed SEDs for Arp220 and M82 were constructed from data available on the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ ) over the 1-1500 m wavelength range. To ensure a meaningful comparison to SK07, we utilize the same photometric data for M82 and Arp 220 (including multiple aperture JHK photometry for M82) such that the only difference lies in the fitting method. Table tab:compare provides the best fit models as obtained by SK07 and SATMC with the models and parameter-parameter confidence contours shown in Figure fig:sk07 .

sources.summary: The paper studies M82's nuclear Fe 6.4 and 6.7 keV emission with deep Chandra data, finding diffuse line emission, 5-6 keV thermal gas, subsolar Fe abundances, and a starburst superwind context.

quotes.quote: We study the spatial distribution of the Fe 6.4 and 6.7 keV lines in the nuclear region of M82 using the \ archival data with a total exposure time of 500 ks. The deep exposure provides a significant detection of the Fe 6.4 keV line. Both the Fe 6.4 and 6.7 keV lines are diffuse emissions with similar spatial extent, but their morphology do not exactly follow each other. Assuming a thermal collisional-ionization-equilibrium (CIE) model, the fitted temperatures are around 5-6 keV and the Fe abundances are about 0.4-0.6 solar value.
The prototype starburst galaxy M82 (located at 3.6 Mpc), with a powerful superwind detected on scales up to 10 kpc, is an ideal target to study the driving plasma of superwinds.

sources.summary: The paper uses NUV, optical, and IR imaging of M82 to study dust in its interstellar medium and superwind, favoring a Milky-Way-type NUV law and recent M81 encounter scenario.

quotes.quote: We combine NUV, optical and IR imaging of the nearby starburst galaxy M82 to explore the properties of the dust both in the interstellar medium of the galaxy and the dust entrained in the superwind. The three NUV filters of Swift /UVOT enable us to probe in detail the properties of the extinction curve in the region around the 2175 \ bump. The NUV colour-colour diagram strongly rules out a ``bump-less'' Calzetti-type law, which can either reflect intrinsic changes in the dust properties or in the star formation history compared to starbursts well represented by such an attenuation law. We emphasize that it is mainly in the NUV region where a standard Milky-Way-type law is preferred over a Calzetti law. The age and dust distribution of the stellar populations is consistent with the scenario of an encounter with M81 in the recent 400 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper uses HST photometry to map M82 star-cluster ages and masses, finding three age peaks and spatial differences that constrain the galaxy’s cluster disruption and star-formation history.

quotes.quote: Using high-resolution, multiple-passband Hubble Space Telescope images spanning the entire optical/near-infrared wavelength range, we obtained a statistically complete sample, U band selected sample of 846 extended star clusters across the disk of the nearby starburst galaxy M82. Based on careful analysis of their spectral energy distributions, we determined their galaxy-wide age and mass distributions. The M82 clusters exhibit three clear peaks in their age distribution, thus defining a relatively young, ( t yr ^ 1 ) 7.5 , an intermediate-age, ( t yr ^ 1 ) [ 7.5, 8.5 ] , and an old sample, ( t yr ^ 1 ) 8.5 . Comparison of the completeness-corrected mass distributions offers a firm handle on the galaxy's star cluster disruption history. The most massive star clusters in the young and old samples are (almost) all concentrated in the most densely populated central region, while the intermediate-age sample's most massive clusters are more spatially dispersed, which may reflect the distribution of the highest-density gas throughout the galaxy's evolutionary history, combined with the solid-body nature of the galaxy's central region.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes M 82 as an evolved, UV-field-dominated starburst whose NE molecular lobe has strong PDR tracers, abundant CH3CCH, and weaker shock tracers than NGC 253.

quotes.quote: The gas in the NE lobe of M 82 is strongly affected by UV fields which shape its chemical composition (e.g. Aladro11b ). Molecular clouds are highly ionized and fragmented Fuente08 and, according to our results, the PDR tracers -C _3 H _2 , HCO, and HOC ^+ have slightly higher abundances than in NGC 253 (but just a factor of two or less. See Table ratios ). The species that shows the largest difference between M 82 and the other two starburst galaxies is CH _3 CCH, being in the former five times more abundant (see Sect. keyspecies for more details). Still, some shock tracers, such as CH _3 OH, SiO, and HNCO, are detectable in the cores of the molecular clouds that are well shielded from the UV radiation Martin06a . However, their abundances are four times lower than in NGC 253, indicating that, although present, shocks do not represent the main heating mechanism of the molecular gas as compared to UV radiation.

sources.summary: The paper describes M82 as a starburst galaxy with UV halo filaments, a more disk-like NUV halo than FUV halo, decreasing FUV-NUV color with height, and large-scale H I not visible in UV.

quotes.quote: For example, in M82 and NGC 253 (Figure figure.gallery_starburst ), the NUV halo appears more like a thick disk than the FUV halo, which is more filamentary in nature. This morphology cannot be explained by PSF-wing contamination or filter artifacts.

In NGC 253, M82, NGC 4631, NGC 3628, NGC 3079, and NGC 4388, the FUV NUV color decreases with height. This is consistent with the FUV NUV color maps (Figure figure.fuvnuv_sb ) and suggests that the UV halos comprise two components.
However, the large-scale hydrogen filaments seen around galaxies in our sample (such as NGC 55, NGC 3628, NGC 4565, NGC 4631, or M82) are not visible in the UV.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 3034 as a gamma-ray bright star-forming galaxy with 8.06 significance after background modeling, and includes it among the four brightest systems used to derive the average spectrum.

quotes.quote: Our analysis confirms previous results indicating that the systems NGC 253, NGC 1068, NGC 3034 and NGC 4945 are ray bright SFGs. Utilizing the distribution of P _ bg to translate the likelihood function of each point source the statistical significance of the source (taking into account the possibility of background mismodeling), we can determine that the ray emission observed from each SFG is statistically significant at confidence levels of: 7.00 (NGC 253), 4.42 (NGC 1068), 8.06 (NGC 3034), and 7.10 (NGC 4945). Because the distribution P _ bg takes into account the possibility of background fluctuations, we note that all of these sources are highly significant.

Using the four brightest systems in our analysis (NGC 253, NGC 1068, NGC 3034 and NGC 4945), we calculate the average ray spectrum above 1 GeV to follow a power-law with a spectral index of = -2.32 0.06. These four systems are all individually best-fit with power-law spectra between -2.2 and -2.4.

sources.summary: The MAGIC paper uses M82's distance, starburst identity, VERITAS gamma-ray flux, and neutral-hydrogen content when interpreting upper limits for SN 2014J.

quotes.quote: It is located in the starburst galaxy M82 at a distance of 3.6 Mpc karachentsev_masses_2006 .

Our ULs for E>700 GeV are already close to the flux from the host galaxy M82 measured by VERITAS in the same energy range, (3.7 0.8_ stat 0.7_ syst ) 10^ 13 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 2009Natur.462..770V , which constitutes an irreducible background for our measurement. Under the hypothesis that M82 has a gamma-ray spectrum of d F/ d E = 3 10^ 16 (E/1000 GeV )^ 2.5 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 GeV ^ 1 , as measured by VERITAS, the expected number of excess events in our observations would be 9.4, with a 95 CL lower limit at -6.1. The observed number of excess events by MAGIC is -4.2, with an associated p-value of 8.4 10^ 2 , hence consistent with the VHE flux of M82 measured by VERITAS.
In this work, we used a density of n=2.2 10^ 24 g/cm ^ 3 , based on , assuming that all the content in the host galaxy of our source, M82, stems from the neutral hydrogen, H _ I .

sources.summary: The survey models M82 as an edge-on starburst with bipolar outflow, weak H2O relative to CO, lower water abundance, and distinct warm and cold gas components.

quotes.quote: M82 M82 is a nearby, almost edge-on galaxy ( i 77 deg), notable for its spectacular bipolar outflow e.g., kamenetzky2012 . High-resolution CO maps of the central 1 kpc disk indicate that the molecular gas is largely concentrated in three areas: a north-east lobe, south-west lobe, and to a lesser extent, a central region weiss2001, weiss2010, walter2002 . Overall, M82 shows weaker H_ 2 O line emission relative to CO.

Our models suggest that the faintness of H _2 O in M82 is mainly due to the lower H_ 2 O abundance (see Table. \ table: model parameters ). Our model for M82 requires a warm and a more extended cold component to match the observed H _2 O line intensities, where the warm gas dominates the medium-excitation H _2 O lines, dust FIR continuum and high- J ( J 8 ) CO lines while the cold gas mainly contributes to the low-excitation H_ 2 O , the long wavelength submm dust continuum and low/middle- J ( J 8 ) CO emission lines.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3034/M 82 as a classical nearby starburst with extensive radio-continuum measurements and high-frequency data selected to avoid low-frequency spectral complications.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 and NGC 3034 (M 82) are classical nearby starburst galaxies, again with the intense star-forming activity resulting from gravitational interaction with a nearby galaxy or from a past merger event.

NGC 3034 (M82) has been the target of a large number of radio continuum studies across a large frequency range. adebahr13 presented observations at 3, 6, 22, and 92 cm, using the VLA and the WSRT. 2015A&A...574A.114V made dedicated measurements with LOFAR to image the radio continuum of M 82 at 118 and 154 MHz. However, such low-frequency measurements are not relevant for our present study, since at frequencies below about 1 GHz various effects may shape the synchrotron spectra in such a way as to produce deviations from a simple power-law (see Sect. subsect:old_obs ).

sources.summary: The paper applies its clustered-supernova wind model to M82, arguing that observed star-cluster masses can plausibly drive breakout and account for M82’s energetic hot wind.

quotes.quote: We now consider the application of our results to the prototypical local starburst M82 and z 2 star forming disc galaxies. M82 has _g 3000 , t_d 2 10^6 yrs, and v 20 1998ApJ...498..541K,Greco2012 . Equations eq:Resc and eq:vesc thus imply R_s(t = ) 5 h and v_s(R_s = h) 20 , such that breakout at t is plausible, but only for our fiducial = 0.03 - 0.1 . The latter corresponds to 0.5-1.5 10^ 6 for our assumed M82 conditions, consistent with the star cluster masses in M82 McCrady2007 . Thus our model argues that clusters like those observed can indeed account for the large energy in the hot wind in M82 inferred from Chandra observations by Strickland2009 .

sources.summary: The paper describes M82 as a starburst galaxy whose outflows disturb the H I velocity field, spread gas over wide velocities, and carry molecular gas far beyond the disk.

quotes.quote: The starburst galaxy M82 is a unique target for studying interaction-triggered star formation feedback processes in essentially all wavelength bands (e.g., H , X-rays, dust, , molecular gas, see walter02a,leroy15 and references therein).

The orientation of the kinematical minor axis of M82 seems to be almost perpendicular to its optical minor axis. It is likely that this is caused by the gas outflows in M82 (e.g., yun93b, walter02a,leroy15,martini18 ) affecting the velocity field. Slices 6 and 7 in Fig. \ fig:slices show that in these regions is present with a velocity spread of close to 400 .
CO observations of M82 show that the molecular gas extends far beyond the disk, with the well-known gas outflows dominating the distribution leroy15 .

sources.summary: M82 has detected N2H+ and is grouped with NGC253; the best models require high cosmic-ray ionization, matching its role as a chemically rich starburst galaxy.

quotes.quote: Aladro et al. (2015) detected N _2 H ^+ in 4 galaxies: M83, NGC253, M82, and M51, and found a column density of 6.5 10 ^ 12 , 4 10 ^ 13 , 1 10 ^ 13 and 4 10 ^ 12 cm ^ 2 , respectively.

Grouping M83 with M51 and M82 with NGC253 (due to their similar values of observed N _2 H ^+ ) we find that for the first two galaxies this translates into a N _2 H ^+ fractional abundance ranging between 2.5 and 4 10 ^ 10 if the visual extinction is 10 mag and 2.5 and 4 10 ^ 11 if the visual extinction is 100 mag.

M82 and NGC253 : at 10 mags the only model that reproduces the observed abundance of N _2 H ^+ is one with a high cosmic ray ionization rate (1000 _o ), a temperature of 100 K and a gas density of 10 ^4 cm ^ 3 , while if the gas is at a visual extinction of 100 mags then the same model but with a factor of 10 less cosmic ray ionization rate can reproduce the observed abundance of N _2 H ^+ . We recall that the derived abundances from the observations are different at different extinctions which is why for this comparison models at different visual extinctions do give different matches. We note that these two galaxies are the prototypical chemically rich starburst galaxies and, again, as for the other two galaxies, a higher than standard cosmic ray ionization rate is expected.

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as a starburst-galaxy case where secondary electrons can explain radio emission, hadronic gamma rays were observed, and propagation uncertainties affect modeled secondary-electron spectra.

quotes.quote: Using pp reaction rates, 1994A&A...287..453P demonstrated that the radio spectrum observed from M82 could be as well explained assuming secondary electrons only as with conventional primary electrons. The corresponding gamma-ray emission from hadronic interactions that one would expect in the former scenario was later observed at the required level 2010ApJ...709L.152A . In both cases a substantial opacity for free-free absorption was required. Naturally there are substantial uncertainties in propagation models for starburst cores, arising from the unknown diffusion properties and escape by advection in the wind, that can lead to some variation in the total particle spectrum of secondary electrons in M82.
This is particularly relevant for starburst galaxies like M82, for which the bulk of the radio synchrotron emission may be produced by secondary electrons.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3034 (M 82) as a dusty star-forming galaxy without clear AGN evidence, detecting radio SNe, SNRs, H II regions, SN 2008iz, and 41.95+575.

quotes.quote: NGC 3034 (M 82) is a dusty star forming galaxy which lacks clear evidence of an active nucleus so far. We detect a mix of SNe, SNRs, and H ii regions, which have been previously identified by (e-)MERLIN (e.g. muxlow94,beswick06,fenech08,fenech10,muxlow10,gendre13a,varenius15 and references therein). Amongst the stellar remnants we find SN 2008iz which is the brightest source in the LeMMINGs images of M 82 and has a peak luminosity of 60 mJy at 1.5 GHz (epoch 19 April 2017). This bright radio source has been interpreted as synchrotron emission due to an expanding SN shock which encounters clumpy dense medium brunthaler10,kimani16 . One of the interesting sources detected in the e- MERLIN map of M 82 is also 41.95+575, which has a double-lobed structure at VLBI resolution and has been decreasing by 8 per cent per year for the last 5 decades.

sources.summary: The paper models M82 with GeV and TeV data, finds proton cutoffs of order 10^2 TeV, links this to internal SNR acceleration, and tests a two-zone SED fit.

quotes.quote: Because the statistics and TS values from the last three bins were less for NGC 253 and M82, we considered combining the three bins to one, based on Abdalla2018 . For each bin of all SEDs, we guaranteed that their TS values were greater than 4. For the energy bin with a TS value < 4, the upper limits of the 95 confidence level were given. All SEDs of each source are shown in Figure Fig1 . The global fit results for each source are given in Table Tab1 . We found that the average value of the power-law spectral index of SBGs is approximately 2.23, which is consistent with the maximum likelihood analysis results of Ackermann2012a and Ajello2020 . For each panel of Figure Fig1 , we provided the differential sensitivities of Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory Bai2019 and the Cherenkov Telescope Array in the northern/southern hemisphere CTA2019 , to predict their likely very-high-energy (VHE) emission in the future. 1.5 Model Introduction The tool, NAIMA , provided a one-zone stationary model and the proton-proton (pp) interaction differential cross-section of PYTHIA 8 Kafexhiu2014 to fit the spectra of the target sources. This tool employs the Markov chain Monte Carlo method, implemented in the emcee package Foreman2013 . We will use NAIMA to complete all the following analyses. The high-energy radiations in SFGs and SBGs are thought to be caused by CRs colliding with the surrounding interstellar medium (ISM) resulting in the generation of neutral pions and other products Abdo2010b,Abdo2010d,Peretti2019 . In addition, the neutral pions can decay into high-energy ray photons Stecker1971,Dermer1986 ; thus, the SBGs and SFGs were considered to be possible ray sources in the local universe Dermer1986,Strong1976,Paglione1996,Blom1999,Domingo2005 . The ray radiation of SBGs and SFGs above 100 MeV is widely regarded to be of hadronic origin; whereas pionic decay is regarded as the dominant mechanism of ray generation inside them e.g., Domingo2005,Rephaeli2010,Tang2014,Abdalla2018,Peretti2019,Wang2018 . Therefore, we assumed that the high-energy radiation of SBGs and SFGs originated from the collective interaction of internal SNRs and molecular cloud gas inside those starburst regions, and that pionic decay is the dominant radiation mechanism for the GeV ray emission of the SBGs and SFGs. For the absorption of extragalactic background light (EBL), here we consider the EBL model from Dominguez2011 in all the following analyses. For SNRs in the Milky Way, the protonic energy distribution of a power-law distribution with an exponential cutoff (ECPL) has been widely used to explain their ray spectra Aharonian2006,Xing2016, Xin2019,Xiang2021a . Here, we assumed that the protonic energy distributions of SNRs within SBGs and SFGs satisfy the same formula, which is as follows: N(E) = N_ 0 ( E E_ 0 )^ exp (- E E_ cutoff , where N_ 0 is the amplitude, E is the particle energy, is the power-law spectral index, and E_ cutoff is the cutoff energy, and E_ 0 = 1 TeV. Model fit Diffusive shock acceleration (DSA), as a predominant acceleration mechanism, is generally used to explain high-energy particle acceleration at SNR shocks of up to approximately 100 TeV or even higher Aharonian2007,Aharonian2011,Morlino2012a . Therefore, here the range of protonic energy was selected from 0.1 GeV to 0.5 PeV e.g., Abdalla2018 . When the protonic energy distributions of the SBGs and SFGs satisfy (1), we used NAIMA to obtain the best-fit results of the SEDs of all sources, as shown in Figure Fig2 . The related best-fit parameters are given in Table Tab2 , where the protons energy budget W_ p was calculated to be above 290 MeV, which is considered as the pion production threshold Abdalla2018 . Except for the hardening spectral components from NGC 1068 and NGC 4945, we found that the one-zone model with ECPL can explain the SEDs of most SBGs and SFGs. However, we found that the first bins of SEDs of NGC 1068, M82, NGC 253, Arp 299, and SMC cannot be well explained; we assumed that the first bins may be from the contributions of the Bremsstrahlung emission and inverse Compton scattering of the primary or secondary electrons of CRs e.g., Tang2014 . h 1.2 Note: The last column is the references of distance, gas density, and redshift of each source, respectively. The column of N_ SNR is SNR's number inside each source. Here we assumed that the gas density of NGC 3424 was 10 cm ^ 3 since its spectrum and photon flux of the global fit were similar to those of NGC 2146. (1) Ackermann2012a , (2) Gao2004 , (3) Greve2006 , (4) Kavanagh2020 , (5) Karachentsev2017 , (6) Kramer2020 , (7) Lamastra2019 , (8) Lopez2018 , (9) Peretti2019 , (10) Roy2010 , (11) Sanders2003 , (12) Sargent1987 , (13) Wang2018 , (14) Tang2017 , (15) Tully2009 , (16) Ahn2012 , (17) Skrutskie2006 , (18) Abazajian2009 ,(19) Meyer2004 , (20) Falco1999 , (21) van2016 ,(22) Izotova1999 , (23) McConnachie2012 ,(24) Paturel2002 Two-zone Model fit for NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 h As can be seen in Figure Fig2 , we found that the one-zone model did not explain the hardening spectral components from NGC 1068 and NGC 4945. Therefore, we considered a two-zone model, which assumes that they have two kinds of different protonic compositions from the two different zones. All protons inside the two zones satisfy the ECPL energy distribution. The two zones contributed to the soft and hard spectral compositions in the 0.1 to 500 GeV band. As shown in Figure Fig3 , by fitting their SEDs, we found that this two-zone model can better explain the hardening components of the SEDs of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 than the one-zone model. Their best-fit results are given in Table Tab3 . h Spectral Feature All sources are classified into three categories, including SBG with the likely contribution from internal AGN (SBG-AGN), SBG without AGN (SBG), and SFR. For SBG-AGN, there are three objects: Circinus Guo2019 , NGC 3424 Peng2019 , and Arp 299 Xi2020 . Here, we first analyzed the spectral features of the three types of sources. For SBGs, we excluded NGC 4945 and NGC 1068 because they cannot be well explained by the one-zone model; we excluded Arp 220 because its fifth energy bin was associated with a higher flux than the fourth energy. By calculating the average values of the spectral index of NGC 253, M82, and NGC 2146, we found that the value was approximately 2.30. For SBG-AGNs and SFRs, their average values of the spectral index were 2.19 and 2.43, respectively. These results suggest that the spectral features of SBGs and SBG-AGNs are hard, and there is not much difference between them; thus, we suggest that the internal hidden AGNs from SBG-AGNs may not have a significant contribution to the GeV high-energy ray emission. The spectrum of M31 is currently hard, though there are only two data points with TS value > 4. We suggest that its particles may be in a primary acceleration stage because the process of particle acceleration from internal SNRs generally requires a long timescale from hundreds to thousands of years after the SNR explosion Yuan2018 . In the current Fermi LAT observation period of approximately 11 years, its particles may not be accelerated to a high-energy band, which makes the E_ cutoff value of the SED of M31 appear at about 0.03 TeV. On the other hand, if its inner particles are at the stage of the late evolution, this also results in a low E_ cutoff in the spectrum for the radiative cooling effect e.g., Brantseg2013,Cox1972,Tang2013,Zeng2019,Xiang2021c . Similarly, the particles within M33 with a low E_ cutoff of approximately 9.64 TeV are likely to be in an early or late evolution stage. Likely Protonic Acceleration Limit within SBGs Owing to the lack of TeV data for most SBGs and SFRs thus far, there is no definitive conclusion about the possible range of E_ cutoff . Currently, there are only two TeV SBGs, including NGC 253 and M82. Based on the simulation of the one-zone model, we found their values of E_ cutoff to be approximately 159.36 TeV and 166.41 TeV, respectively. These results indicate that their internal protons can be accelerated to the order of 10 ^ 2 TeV, which is close to the particle acceleration limit of current SNRs in the Milky Way Aharonian2007,Aharonian2011,Morlino2012a . Berezhko2003 analyzed the X-ray data of SN 1006 from Chandra observations and confirmed the magnetic field amplification, which can effectively accelerate nuclear CRs in SNRs to TeV or PeV levels Tang2013,Lin2019 . Similar to the SNRs in the Milky Way, if the SBGs contain numerous SNRs and the energy of CR particles in these SNRs can be accelerated to the 10 ^ 2 TeV level, then there should be a strong magnetic field amplification near the shock surface in SNRs Lin2019 . If the evolution of CR particles in NGC 253 and M82 is similar to that of SNRs in the Milky Way, then the magnetic field amplification is likely to be an important mechanism for the acceleration of particles inside them to the 10 ^ 2 TeV level. To derive the limitations of particle acceleration, more high-energy data are expected in the future (e.g., continuous Fermi LAT observations). According to the previous works e.g, Zeng2019,Xiang2021a,Xiang2021c , in the Milky Way, for the hadronic scenario, each SNR needs the average value of W_ p to be approximately 10 ^ 49 erg in the high-energy band. Based on the value of W_ p , we estimated the number of SNR in each source in Table Tab2 . Two-zone SED for NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 There are three SBGs, including NGC 3424, Circinus, and Arp 299, which are considered to have the contribution of AGNs in the GeV band Guo2019,Peng2019,Xi2020 . The main reason is that some variability is found in their light curves of approximately ten years. Thus, the contribution of AGNs cannot be ignored for these SBGs. The correlation of the X-ray and ray emission of NGC 4945 indicated that the ray emission was likely dominated by AGN Wojaczynski2017 . Moreover, the ray emission of NGC 1068 was believed to be likely from its AGN activity, considering that it exceeded the expectation of the star-forming process Lenain2010,Ackermann2012a,Eichmann2016 . However, The GeV flare is an important feature of AGN and has not been observed from NGC 4945 and NGC 1068 thus far Ackermann2012a,Peng2019,Ajello2020 . In addition, they well conformed to the two well-known luminosity relations; one is that of the total IR (8-1000 m) and the ray luminosities; the other is that of the ray and 1.4 GHz radio continuum luminosities. These results suggest that their ray emissions may originate from the star-forming process Yun2001,Thompson2007, Ackermann2012a, Guo2019, Xi2020, Ajello2020 . The previous research results provide strong evidence to support the hadronic origin of SNR for NGC 4945 and NGC 1068 in this analysis. For SNRs, Uchiyama2007 found the variable X-ray filaments (or knots) within the region of RX J1713.7-3946 with Suzaku and Chandra. These filaments themselves could contribute significantly to the ray emission from RX J1713.7-3946 Finke2012 . This result implies that the one-zone fit is inadequate to explain the overall SEDs of SNRs in the Milky Way. Therefore, Finke2012 considered a multi-zone scenario to well explain the multi-band SED of RX J1713.7-3946. Furthermore, Zhang2016 used a two-zone model to well fit the broadband spectrum of RX J1713.7-3946. In their model, the first zone was the cavity wall of SNR with the dense matter at the cavity boundary; the second zone was inside the SNR. Lu2020 considered a two-zone model with different diffusion processes from the extended region of SNR and the internal pulsar wind nebula region to explain the broadband SED of SNR G21.5-0.9. Similarly, Xiang2021b also used a simple two-zone model, irrespective of the acceleration process of CR particles, to well explain overall SED from composite SNR G327.1+1.1. If the GeV emissions of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 are two-zone collective contributions from their internal SNRs, we found that the two-zone model can well explain the hardening phenomena of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 at the end of their GeV spectra. Since NGC 253 and M82 have GeV and TeV data points, we also used the two-zone model to fit their SEDs to verify that the model is generally useful for explaining the high-energy origin of current SBGs. The best-fit results of NGC 253 and M82 are shown in Figure Fig3 . We find that the two-zone model can also explain the GeV to TeV SEDs of the two SBGs. In Table Tab3 , for the four SBGs, we can see that the spectra of hadronic component 1 are harder than those of hadronic component 2; the values of W_ p of the former are one or two orders of magnitude lower than those of the latter. In the future, more observations with high precision in the high-energy band are required to confirm the two-zone hypothesis. 1. We regenerated the SEDs of nine SBGs and four SFGs using Fermitools. Owing to the uncertainty of the acceleration mechanism inside the SBGs and SFGs, we used the simple one-zone model provided by NAIMA to explain their GeV spectra. Moreover, we considered the two-zone model to better explain the hardening components from the spectra of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 than the one-zone scenario. 2. Classifying all sources into three categories, we found that the spectral features of SBGs and SBG-AGNs were not significantly different from the average spectral indexes of 2.30 and 2.19, respectively. The SFG spectra were relatively soft, with an average spectral index of 2.43. 3. Analyzing the E_ cutoff of these sources, we found that the E_ cutoff of NGC 253 and M82 attained the order of 10 ^ 2 TeV, which indicates that the internal protons can be accelerated to the order of 10 ^ 2 TeV in the one-zone scenario.

sources.summary: The paper models M82's diffuse X-ray starburst wind, using observed non-spherical outflow geometry to infer mass loading, lower hot-wind velocities, and limb-brightening from edge entrainment.

quotes.quote: We then model the diffuse X-ray emission from the local starburst M82. The non-spherical (more cylindrical) outflow geometry is directly taken from the observed X-ray surface brightness profile. These models imply a total mass-loading rate that is about equal to that injected in the starburst, 10 M _ yr ^ 1 , and they predict an asymptotic hot wind velocity of 1000 km \ s^ 1 that is 1.5-2 times smaller than previous predictions. We also show how the observed entropy profile can be used to constrain the outflow velocity, making predictions for future missions like XRISM. We argue that the observed X-ray limb-brightening may be explained by mass-loading at the outflow's edges.

sources.summary: The review describes M82 as an exceptional local dwarf with a developed, multiphase galactic wind that illustrates extreme outflow properties.

quotes.quote: Dwarf galaxy winds The presence of large-scale gaseous outflows have long been inferred from observations of dwarf galaxies and an exceptional example is the majestic M82 system ( ; and see Fig. fig:m82 ), the only dwarf galaxy in the local Universe that exhibits a developed and powerful wind. While something of an outlier, M82 helps to demonstrate the possible characteristics of an extreme wind. Galactic winds are cone-like in shape, moving at velocities 25-100 kms ^ 1 perpendicular to the dwarf galaxy's stellar and gaseous disc . They are also multi-phase, typically containing hot ( T>10^5 K ), warm ( 10^3<T<10^5 K ) and cold ( T<10^3 K ) components that entrain metals, molecules and dust . While M82 presents us with a very obvious detection of winds, identifying and catagorising winds in dwarf galaxies is observationally challenging.

The hot phase is thought to be the most energetic and most metal-enhanced component. It has the greatest chance of escaping the disk and even halo of the galaxy, enriching the CGM zheng19 , as shown for M82 . The presence of diffuse soft X-ray emission beyond the HI disk is thus typically interpreted as an outflow event. Unfortunately, due to the low surface brightness of the emission, X-ray observations (both hard and soft) are typically limited to the most energetic starburst systems (such as M82, NGC 4449, NCG 625, NGC 1569, NGC 4214, ), and are likely to fade just 25 Myr after the starburst that generated them .

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as a starburst model galaxy, finding its observed cosmic-ray density and star-formation rate still below cosmic-ray Eddington requirements.

quotes.quote: We can check the reliability of our formulae by applying it to the starburst core of M82, for which many of the relevant parameters are observable or constrained by independent modeling. Applying eqn. ( eq:ksLaw ) for the parameters given in Table 1, we predict an SFR of 4.9 M_ /yr, which is lower by a factor of 2 than the accepted value of 10 M_ /yr. Using the Milky Way value, = 3 10^ 28 cm^2 s^ 1 StrongCRProp2007 in eqn. ( eq:Uc0fromKS ) and again taking z=200 pc gives U_ c0 =210 eV cm ^ 3 , also about a factor of 2 less than the best fit value of 525 eV cm ^ 3 obtained in , agreement we regard as satisfactory given the many assumptions underlying the analysis and models.

The galaxy that gets the closest to its observed star formation rate is M82. We find that M82's Eddington star formation rate is about five times larger than its actual star formation rate, while the next closest, NGC 4449, would have to have a star formation rate about eleven times its observed value to be super-Eddington.

sources.summary: M82 is discussed as an interacting M81-group galaxy whose nearby globular clusters have distinct velocities and are likely not yet mixed with the wider M81-group cluster population.

quotes.quote: Four old GCs in M82 galaxy have been explored using spectroscopy; two from Saito05 and two from Konstantopoulos2009 . used HST images to identify star clusters in M82; 35 of them had colours red enough to be identified as candidate GCs.

fig:radial velocity is that the six GCs closest to M82 have a higher mean radial velocity (141.22 12.10 km/s) than that of M81, or the mean velocity of M81's GCs from NH10 (mean v_r = -23 4 km/s). The chance of drawing 6 GCs with a higher mean velocity than those near M82 from the NH10 velocity distribution is about 0.27 . This implies the M82 halo GCs are likely to have belonged to M82 and have not yet kinematically mixed with the other M81 group GCs.

sources.summary: The paper finds M82's observed gamma-ray spectrum is nearly reproduced by the M3e11-spec simulation, not by a more massive halo snapshot, and discusses mass estimates affecting inferred halo mass.

quotes.quote: Similarly, the spectrum of M82 is almost perfectly reproduced by our spectrally resolved CR proton simulation M3e11-spec (right-hand panel of Fig. fig: gamma-ray spectra NGC2146,M82 ), where again the steady-state spectra are softer at high gamma-ray energies. However, we find that the observed spectrum of M82 is only matched with our M3e11-spec simulation but not with a corresponding snapshot of the more massive halo M1e12-spec where the simulated galaxy exhibits a hard spectrum above 10 GeV in contradiction to the data. A different estimation for the stellar mass of M82 obtained via the relation given by 2013Cappellari (which assumes that the dynamical mass within the observed region is M_ ) using the K-band apparent magnitude m_K = 4.665 2006Skrutskie and a distance of 3.5 Mpc (3.7 Mpc) yields a stellar mass of M_ 4.1 10^ 10 ( 4.5 10^ 10 ) and a halo mass of 1.4 10^ 12 ( 1.6 10^ 12 ), where we used the relation by 2010Moster to infer the latter. However, 2012Greco estimate the dynamical mass of M82 (out to a radius of 4 kpc) to be only 10^ 10 . If one assumes this to be an estimate for the stellar mass, this would imply a much smaller halo mass of 4.5 10^ 11 .

sources.summary: The review describes M82 as a nearby starburst with ordered superwind fields, detected gamma-ray emission, and gamma-ray spectra modeled as mainly hadronic with partial cosmic-ray ion escape.

quotes.quote: Data from SOFIA/HAWC+ in combination with a potential field extrapolation show a dominant large-scale ordered poloidal field associated with the galactic superwind of two nearby starburst galaxies M82 and NGC 253 jones_sofia_2019,lopez-rodriguez_strength_2021 .

Early on, it has been realized that the nearby starburst galaxies M82 and NGC 253 with their dense ISM ( n 300  cm ^ 3 ) and high CR energy densities as inferred from the high radio luminosity, voelk_correlation_1989 should also be bright ray sources akyuz_m_1991,sreekumar_study_1994,volk_nonthermal_1996,paglione_diffuse_1996,romero_signatures_2003,torres_luminous_2004,domingo-santamaria_high_2005,thompson_starburst_2007,persic_very-high-energy_2008,de_cea_del_pozo_multimessenger_2009,rephaeli_high-energy_2010 , and that this argument should also carry over to starburst galaxies in general pohl_predictive_1994,torres_luminous_2004 . These theoretical predictions were confirmed by the detection of ray emission from M82 and NGC 253 with Fermi abdo_detection_2010 and imaging air Cerenkov telescopes acero_detection_2009,the_veritas_collaboration_connection_2009 .

Modeling the ray spectra in one-zone models and comparing them to observational data of M82 and NGC 253 argues for the ray emission that is mainly of hadronic origin see figure 1 of for a compilation of several previous studies lacki_gev_2011 . Using cooling time arguments and the observed ray fluxes, lacki_gev_2011 suggest that despite efficient pion losses of CR ions in both galaxies, a fraction of 0.6--0.8 of high-energy primary CR ions can escape before hadronically interacting with the ISM.

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as a nearby starburst-outflow comparison, noting flat hot-gas metal profiles, southern approaching outflow geometry, abundance consistency with NGC 4945 and NGC 253, and higher receding-outflow abundances.

quotes.quote: The analysis in this paper follows that performed in of M82 ( D = 3.6  Mpc) as well as in of NGC 253 ( D = 3.5  Mpc), two other nearby galaxies with starburst-driven outflows. showed that the M82 and NGC 253 hot gas outflows contain the same metals, but their distributions differ with distance from the galactic disks, with the former profiles being flat, while the latter are more centrally peaked.

The approaching outflows in M82 and NGC 253 are the southern outflows, while for NGC 4945 it is the northern outflow. In the approaching outflows, the abundances of NGC 4945, NGC 253, and M82 are statistically consistent with each other where constrained. We find that M82 has higher abundances in the receding outflow for all measured elements (note: oxygen was not constrained in the M82 spectral fits because of the high column density).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM-Newton observations of M82, presenting it as the prototype starburst galaxy for studying charge-exchange emission and multiwavelength biconical outflows from its nuclear region.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the XMM-Newton observations of M82, a prototype starburst galaxy, aiming to investigate these key properties of the CX emisssion.

M82, the prototype of a starburst galaxy, is where CX emission was detected for the first time Ranalli2008 . Because of its proximity 3.6 Mpc Freedman1994 and large inclination angle i 80^ ; McKeith1995 , M82 is regarded as the ideal target for observational studies on outflows. Biconical outflows from the nuclear region of M82 extend perpendicularly to the galactic disc and their properties have been widely studied across the electromagnetic spectrum, tracing atomic H 1 and molecular gas , warm-ionized gas in H , hot plasma emitting X-rays e.g., Watson1984,Lopez2020 , and dust in the UV, IR, and submillimeter bands . Past observations of M82 aiming to investigate the nature of X-rays pointed out the discrepancy between the plasma model and the CCD spectra in the O 7 He triplet in both the galactic disk and outflow regions of M82 Konami2011,Lopez2020 .

sources.summary: The paper treats M 82 as one of the M 81 group’s two gravitationally dominant galaxies, interacting with M81 and NGC 3077, with measured luminosity and velocity used in group-dynamics context.

quotes.quote: The central region of the group is characterized by three major galaxies (M81, M82 and NGC 3077) that are interacting with each other, as demonstrated by a complex HI network of filaments, tidal tails and candidate tidal dwarf galaxies 1994Natur.372..530Y,2008AJ....135.1983C,2008ApJ...689..160W .

We consider galaxies with <-18.0 in absolute magnitudes in the band as satellites. This effectively removes the two gravitationally dominant galaxies M 81 and M 82 from the list, but still includes the bright galaxies NGC 2976 (with M_r=-18.0 mag) and NGC 3077 (with M_r=-17.8 mag). M 81 and M 82 have stellar luminosities (derived from the K_s band) of 10^ 10.95 M _ and 10^ 10.59 M _ 2013AJ....145..101K , respectively.
For example, M 81 has a line-of-sight velocity of -38 km s ^ 1 1978ApJ...223..391D and M 82 of 183 km s ^ 1 1981MNRAS.195..327A .

sources.summary: The paper treats M82 as a marginal AME detection: statistical tests favor the AME model, but the embedded signal is weak and its peak is near 30 GHz.

quotes.quote: Combining with the ancillary data, we investigated the radio-IR spectral energy distribution (SED), while searching for the AME signals in five galaxies. The AME in NGC 2903 was well detected at a significant confidence level, whereas that in NGC 2146 and M82 was marginal. NGC 1068 and Arp 299 indicated no significant hints, and we provided the upper limits for the AME.

The statistical tests suggested that among the sample of five, NGC 2146, NGC 2903, and M82 favored the model with the AME component, which is supported by the AIC and the LR statistical tests with p-values < 0.05. Although the ^2_ n values deny the favor of the model with AME, as evident, the AME-included model described the observed SED better than the model without the AME component. This was particularly observed in the essential frequencies for the AME component, which is supported by smaller ^2 values in the AME-included model cases. In addition, results based on the convolved data are provided; however, the data of the original resolutions yielded a consistent result, favoring the model with AME in the same three targets. Although there are three galaxies for which the presence of AME was suggestive based on statistical tests, the AME in NGC 2146 and M82 were classified as marginal detections owing to the AME component being embedded in total SED and having a small signal-to-noise ratio, S/N_ AME 2.0 .

Meanwhile, the peak of M82's AME was obtained at a more comparable _ peak with those reported both in Galactic and extragalactic objects, that is, 30 GHz. Thus, at least two among our three detections may originate from the region of higher temperature and/or density, whereas AME in M82 arises under similar conditions as M31 and Galactic regions or other nearby galaxies e.g., Tibbs_2012, Murphy_2010 .

sources.summary: The paper considers M 82 as the possible origin of the foreground gas, noting velocities matching the M 81-M 82 tidal tail and a scenario where M 82 lost disk material in an encounter.

quotes.quote: The origin of the gas on our line of sight is possibly M 82 . The absorption velocities are comparable to the ones found by Yun et al. ( ) for the H i tidal tail connecting M 81 and M 82 . A recent simulation by Sofue ( ) indicates that M 82 could have lost its complete disk in an encounter with M 81 .

Another explanation for the observed level of ionisation could be the presence of a stellar component in the gas, not unrealistic in a scenario of material ripped from M 82 in a close encounter. If M 82 indeed lost its spiral arms, stars could well be present in the intergalactic medium of the M 81 group.

sources.summary: The paper reports ammonia detections in M 82, deriving cool dense molecular gas, a temperature rise toward the nucleus, and declining ammonia abundance in central active regions.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of the (J,K) = (1,1), (2,2), and (3,3) inversion lines of ammonia ( ) towards the south--western molecular lobe in M 82. The relative intensities of the ammonia lines are characterized by a rotational temperature of T_ rot = 29 5 K which implies an average kinetic temperature of T_ kin 60 K. A Gaussian decomposition of the observed spectra indicates increasing kinetic temperatures towards the nucleus of M 82, consistent with recent findings based on CO observations.

The observations imply a very low \ abundance relative to , X( ) 5 10^ 10 . We present evidence for a decreasing \ abundance towards the central active regions in M 82 and interpret this abundance gradient in terms of photodissociation of \ in PDRs.

sources.summary: The review presents M82 as a nearby starburst with intense central synchrotron emission, cosmic-ray and magnetic energy densities far above the Milky Way, and strong far-infrared radiation from its dense starburst nucleus.

quotes.quote: M82 M82 is the small sister of the large spiral M81 in the Northern Hemisphere, at a declination of 70 degrees. The high radio synchrotron intensity in the central region (galactocentric radius 300 pc; height above disk midplane 100 pc) of M82 suggests very high CR intensities. From minimum energy arguments we have a total CR energy density u_ CR 80 eV/cm ^3 and a magnetic field strength B 50 G, assuming an electron to proton ratio of 10^ 2 , similar to that in the Galaxy. Thus, u_ CR and the magnetic field energy density u_B may exceed their values in the Galaxy ( 1 eV/cm ^3 ) by about two orders of magnitude. Also the far infrared (FIR) emission is large with an energy density u_ rad 200 eV/cm ^3 u_ rad u_B in the disk, presumably due to the absorption of the UV radiation from the many young and massive stars by interstellar dust in the high mean (molecular) gas density 140 H-atoms/cm ^3 of the SB nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper uses prior extinction and Lyman-continuum results for the NGC 3034 starburst core and describes its elongated MIR source.

quotes.quote: NGC 3034 (M 82) - We used directly the extinction and Q_ Lyc results of FS01 for the starburst core within d = 30 . These results were derived from an extensive set of H lines from optical to radio wavelengths which are best fitted by a mixed model and with deviations from the Dra89 extinction law, at = 3 - 10 m, as found towards the Galactic Center Lut99a . The MIR source is elongated along the optical major axis. Computing the quadratic mean of the major axis and minor axis widths, we find that 2.5 HPBW = 29.95 \ at 15 m, thus extremely close to the adopted aperture of 30 . The 5-8.5 m emission is more extended, and we would have derived an aperture of 35 \ from it.

sources.summary: The paper uses M82's fossil starburst region B as evidence that intermediate-age cluster luminosity and mass functions can evolve toward old globular-cluster-like systems.

quotes.quote: We recently reported the first discovery of an approximately lognormal CLF (and CMF) for the star clusters in M82's fossil starburst region ``B'', formed roughly simultaneously in a pronounced burst of cluster formation (de Grijs et al. 2003b; see also Goudfrooij et al. 2004). This provides the very first sufficiently deep CLF (and CMF) for a star cluster population at intermediate age (of 1 Gyr), which thus serves as an important benchmark for theories of the evolution of star cluster systems. The CLF shape and characteristic luminosity of the M82 B cluster system is nearly identical to that of the apparently universal CLFs of the old GC systems in the local Universe.

With the very short characteristic cluster disruption time-scale governing M82 B (de Grijs et al. 2003c), its cluster mass distribution will evolve toward a higher characteristic mass scale than that of the Galactic GC system by the time it reaches a similar age. Thus, this evidence, combined with the similar cluster sizes (de Grijs et al. 2001), lends strong support to a scenario in which the current M82 B cluster population will eventually evolve into a significantly depleted old Milky Way-type GC system dominated by a small number of high-mass clusters (de Grijs et al. 2003b).

sources.summary: The review describes GALEX results on M82 halo UV filaments, interpreting most halo UV light as scattered starburst continuum and noting dust-bearing material possibly stripped from M82 or M81.

quotes.quote: NGC 0253 and M82 In a study of the complex of ultraviolet filaments in the starburst-driven halos of NGC 0253 and M82 Hoopes et al. concluded that the UV luminosities in the halo are too high to be provided by continuum and line emission from shock-heated or photoionized gas except perhaps in the brightest filaments in M82. This suggests that most of the UV light is the stellar continuum of the starburst scattered into our line of sight by dust in the outflow. This interpretation agrees with previous results from optical imaging polarimetry in M82.

UV light is seen in the `` \ cap'' 11 kpc north of M82. If this cap is a result of the wind fluid running into a pre-existing gas cloud, the gas cloud contains dust and is not primordial in nature but was probably stripped from M82 or M81 (Figure 2).

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as a classical luminous starburst comparison, noting its superwind, hot central region, abundance indications, and X-ray line diagnostics.

quotes.quote: M82 is a classical luminous starburst galaxy that has been studied by all X-ray satellites (Read Stevens 2002 and references therein). The galaxy is known to have a prominent superwind most clearly seen in H_ images as well as in X-ray lines (e.g. Stevens, Read and Bravo-Guerrero, 2003). The \ based work of Read and Stevens and Stevens et al. claim a two region superwind with indications for a hotter central region. They also suggested abundances that deviate from solar and some contribution to the observed high energy continuum from a known point source (possibly a medium-size black hole) and perhaps several unresolved X-ray binaries. The starburst origin of most of the observed X-ray emission is clearly indicated by the many iron charge states and by the line ratios in several He-like ions (Read and Stevens 2002).

sources.summary: The paper treats M 82 as a nearby northern starburst galaxy, citing model TeV flux estimates and EGRET reanalysis upper limits for its gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The expected very high energy ray emission from this object is discussed in the framework of a galactic wind propagation model. gamma-rays: observations -- Galaxies: starburst -- Galaxies: individual objects: NGC 253 ; Starburst galaxies, such as the closest objects of this type, NGC 253 in the Southern and M 82 in the Northern Hemisphere, are known to exhibit enhanced and strongly localised supernova explosion rates in so called 'starburst regions'.

Given the paradigm of cosmic-ray (CR) acceleration in supernova remnants (SNR), substantially greater CR energy densities E_ 100 E_ ^ gal (where E_ ^ gal 1 eV cm ^ 3 is the value in our galaxy) are expected in such regions, with corresponding fluxes for ^ 0 decay rays alone in the range of 10^ 9 F_ (>300 MeV ) 10^ 8 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 for an object such as M 82 (V olk et al. ). Later estimates for M 82 (Aky uz et al. ; V olk et al. ) are approximately encompassed by this range, which translates into a ray flux of 1.5 10^ 13 F_ ( > 1 TeV ) (E_ /1 TeV )^ 1.1 1.5 10^ 12 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at energies E_ 300 MeV assuming a differential proton energy spectrum E_ p ^ 2.1 .

More complete EGRET data led Blom et al. ( ) to revise this upper limit down to 3.4 10^ 8 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ; for M 82 they derive an upper limit of 4.4 10^ 8 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . Similar upper limits for both NGC 253 and M 82 were obtained in a recent reanalysis of the EGRET data by Cillis et al. ( ).

sources.summary: The paper describes M82 as a nearby giant starburst galaxy whose tidal-triggered nuclear burst, visible starburst regions, super star clusters, and high-pressure gas shape cluster evolution.

quotes.quote: M82 is the best nearby example of a giant starburst galaxy at a distance of 3.6 Mpc (Freedman et al. 1994). The present intense burst of nuclear star formation was triggered by a tidal interaction with M81 some 2 -5 10^8 yr ago (Brouillet et al. 1991; Yun, Ho Lo 1994). The active starburst region has a diameter of 500 pc and is defined optically by the high surface brightness regions or clumps denoted A, C and E by O'Connell Mangano (1978). An optical imaging study of the central region of M82 with the Planetary Camera onboard the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) by O'Connell et al. (1995) revealed the presence of over one hundred candidate SSCs within the visible starburst.

The diffuse gas in region A has a slightly lower pressure, which together with the broad H emission line width, suggests that both the thermal and turbulent pressures in the M82 starburst core are unusually high.

sources.summary: The review describes M82 as a nearby star-forming galaxy rich in compact radio supernova remnants, with VLBI resolving central sources and motivating radio studies of obscured starbursts.

quotes.quote: In many nearby galaxies with high star-formation rates, such as M82 and Arp220 numerous compact radio sources are detected . For example, in M82 approaching 50 compact objects are detected at centremetric wavelengths, the majority of these sources are identified as supernova remnants, the remaining 1 3 are compact H ii regions . Such sources as M82 and Arp220 provide an illustrative example of why radio observations are a vital addition to the numerous studies of supernovae at other wavelengths.

Supernova remnants in M82 As has previously been mentioned the central starburst region within the nearby irregular galaxy M82 contains a wealth of compact sources that are the consequence of the massive and ongoing star-formation within the centre of this source. The two most compact sources in M82 (41.95+475 and 43.31+592) have been resolved in detail using a series of global VLBI observations over the last 20 years .

sources.summary: The paper presents Hubble ACS four-filter mosaics of M82, describing it as the nearest giant starburst galaxy and documenting data processing, registration, drizzling, artifact rejection, and archive release.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: coordinates for M82 given by the NASA Extragalactic Database (in decimal degrees ra = 148.967580, dec = 69.679704 )

quotes.quote: In March 2006, the Hubble Heritage Team obtained a large 4-filter (B, V, I, and H ) 6-point mosaic dataset of the prototypical starburst galaxy NGC 3034 ( M 82 ), with the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) onboard the Hubble Space Telescope (HST). The resulting color composite Heritage image was released in April 2006, to celebrate Hubble's 16th anniversary. Cycle 15 HST proposers were encouraged to submit General Observer and Archival Research proposals to complement and/or analyze this unique dataset. Since our M 82 mosaics represent a significant investment of expert processing beyond the standard archival products, we will also release our drizzle-combined FITS data as a High-Level Science Product via the Multimission Archive at STScI (MAST) in December 2006.

M82 hosts the nearest (D=3.6 Mpc) example of a giant starburst galaxy, with many key processes, such as super star clusters, ISM interfaces, and outflows, occurring on scales that benefit from Hubble's high angular resolution for their study.

sources.summary: The paper treats M 82 as a starburst example with a flat overall synchrotron index, halo spectral steepening, important energy losses, and convection likely shaping its radio spectrum.

quotes.quote: A good example is the starburst galaxy M 82 . For this galaxy the observed overall synchrotron spectral index _ nth = 0.66 (Niklas et al. ) indicates strong convection (see Fig. 2) whereas at the same time the steepening of the spectral index in the halo shows the presence of important energy losses (Seaquist Odegard ; Reuter et al. ).

However, even in this case this model is not able to account for the flatter spectra that some of the galaxies (e.g. M 82 ) show because it is based on diffusion. In this paper, we have shown, that for galaxies with such a low spectral index, convection is likely to be important. With respect to the question of whether confinement or escape is taking place the overall spectral index is not a good diagnostic. Spatially resolved observations are necessary and these indicate that energy losses indeed do play a dominant role. Thus, even for galaxies with a low overall spectral index, like M 82 , escape seems to be negligible as predicted by the calorimeter model.

sources.summary: The paper cites M82 as a starburst galaxy whose fast-variable compact X-ray source indicates an accreting intermediate-mass black hole formed in a starburst environment.

quotes.quote: Fast variability of compact X-ray source in the starburst galaxy M82 indicates that this source is an accreting BH . With the observed luminosity of 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , a mass of the supposed BH is of the order of 10^3 M _ provided the emission is isotropic and corresponds to the Eddington limit. The IMBH in M82 exists in starburst environment. This is a hint on a possible formation of the IMBH in the M82 by a runaway merging of stelar mass BHs .

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as the benchmark starburst-wind system, describing its filamentary minor-axis outflow, asymmetry, and likely entrained disk or halo gas.

quotes.quote: The best studied galactic wind is the outflow in M82, which is clearly visible in the light of H , displaying a vast filamentary system extending several kpc along the minor axis of the galaxy SB1998 . These filaments lie on the surface of a mostly hollow structure and rotate in the same direction as the disk Greve2004 . As with other galactic winds e.g. NGC 253: SDW2003 , the wind in M82 is asymmetric, with the northern outflow more chaotic than the southern outflow. The filaments can be traced to the nuclear region and display both shell and loop-like structures Oetal2002 .

sources.summary: The paper treats M82's Region B as a debated low-extinction view into the disk, arguing its cluster population formed during a galaxy-wide starburst rather than a distinct episode.

quotes.quote: Region B, in the local starburst galaxy M82, has been the object of debate in recent years, due to claims that it hosts a 1 Gyr-old, independent starburst region RdG01 .

This coincidence of SF peak and spread in measured ages between region B and the full galaxy sample reinforces our suggestion that this region is not `special', but is simply located behind a line of sight of relatively low extinction. It has been claimed in the past that region B constitutes a bound region, that moves independently to the rest of the galaxy disk RdG01,RdG03a .

Our data strongly suggest that region B simply represents a line of sight into the galaxy, allowing a clear view of the cluster population of the disk, which undoubtedly formed during a galaxy-wide starburst.

sources.summary: The paper uses M 82 in an HNCO/CS diagnostic, finding low HNCO abundance consistent with UV-dominated, late-stage PDR chemistry and Galactic-center PDR-like molecular abundances.

quotes.quote: Additionally, compared to other potentially good tracers of shock driven chemistry such as CH _3 OH, which has been easily detected towards a number of starburst galaxies Henkel97 and recently also towards M 82 Martin06a , HNCO shows much simpler spectral features, which is fundamental when dealing with extragalactic broad line profiles with typical linewidths of a few hundred km s ^ 1 , as well as a higher abundance contrast than CH _3 OH.

The upper limit to the HNCO abundance of M 82 clearly locate this galaxy in the region of UV dominated material. This agrees well with the finding from other molecules like HCO towards this galaxy Burillo02 suggesting M 82 as the best case of late stage PDR dominated galaxy Martin06b .

A similarly large variation in the HNCO abundance is observed with sources such as M 82 and NGC 253 showing abundances similar to the galactic PDRs while, on the other end, Maffei 2 appears to be more similar to the Galactic center GMCs.

sources.summary: The paper presents M 82 as a nearby starburst galaxy where high-J CS detections reveal multiple molecular gas components, including dense warm CS(7-6) gas associated with its expanding supershell.

quotes.quote: M 82 represents an excellent example of a nearby (D=3.25 Mpc, see Dumk01 ) starburst galaxy.

In summary, we have presented the first detection of a high density tracer (CS) in the Antennae galaxies (NGC 4038 nucleus) as well as the first detections of two high-J transitions of CS in the center of M 82. We find that multiple molecular gas components in M 82 are necessary to explain the observed line intensities. In particular, while low-J CS lines seem to arise from relatively low density gas ( 10^ 5 cm ^ 3 ), the molecular gas traced by the CS(7-6) line must be dense ( 10^ 7 cm ^ 3 ) and warm ( 70K) and appears to be associated with the expanding supershell in M 82.

sources.summary: The paper reports significant Fermi LAT GeV emission from M 82, notes TeV detections, and links its enhanced gamma-ray luminosity to star formation and cosmic-ray energy density.

quotes.quote: For the first time, steady GeV gamma-ray emission has been detected significantly by the LAT from sources positionally coincident with locations of the starburst galaxies M 82 and NGC 253 . Test statistic maps obtained with LAT for photons 200 MeV for regions around both galaxies are shown in Fig. fig:starbursts . Both starburst galaxies have also been detected at TeV energies by VERITAS and H.E.S.S. , and the emission is well explained by the interaction of CRs with local interstellar gas and radiation fields. M 82 and NGC 253, though having less gas than the Milky Way, have factors of 2-4 greater gamma-ray luminosity, suggesting a connection between active star formation and enhanced CR energy densities in star-forming galaxies.

sources.summary: The review presents M82 as a classic starburst in an interacting group, with many O stars, debated cluster IMFs, and an early, well-studied galactic wind.

quotes.quote: M82 is one of the best-known starburst galaxies, with L_ IR 6 10^ 10 L _ , and an estimated 10^4 - 10^5 O stars ( N_ Lyc 8 10^ 54 s^ 1 ).

Rieke et al. \ (1980) 1980ApJ...238...24R modeled the starburst in M82 from its IR emission properties, and concluded that the IMF of the starburst must have a low mass cutoff of 3--8 M _ . Sternberg (1998) used visible mass-to-light ratios to argue for a cutoff of 1 M _ for NGC1705-1, although he does not find a cutoff for NGC 1569A. If the IMF in the M82 SSCs is Salpeter, then McCrady et al. \ (2003) 2003ApJ...596..240M find that the individual M82 clusters must have truncated mass functions, although some clusters show strong evidence for mass segregation and possible dynamical evolution, which complicates this interpretation 2003ApJ...596..314M, 2005ApJ...620L..27B, 2005ApJ...621..278M, 2007ApJ...663..844M .
The starburst galaxy, M82, has one of the earliest known and best studied examples of a galactic wind.

sources.summary: The paper treats M 82 as a starburst comparison nucleus, noting its low HNCO/CS ratio and attributing this to UV-affected nuclear gas and lower GMC column densities.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, as extragalactic prototypical sources, we selected three starburst nuclei in different evolutionary stages ( NGC 253 , M 82 , and NGC 4945 ), and a normal spiral galaxy more similar to the Milky Way ( IC 342 ).

According to Mar09b , this could be the reason the HNCO/CS ratio show a wide range of values toward extragalactic nuclei, from the upper limit of 0.03 in M 82 NE to the high values (0.8--1.0) found toward IC 342 and NGC 4945 , with the ratio in NGC 253 in between (0.25; see Table tab6 ). The low ratio measured toward the GMCs of M 82 is the result of large amounts of gas being affected by UV radiation in its nuclear region. This is due to the lower column densities that M 82 GMCs present in comparison with GMCs of other extragalactic nuclei, where higher HNCO/CS ratios are found Mar09b .

sources.summary: The paper discusses PAHs in M82's bipolar starburst outflow, arguing that hot gas destroys PAHs rapidly unless they are shielded in cooler cloudlets entrained in the galactic wind.

quotes.quote: In our previous work (MJTb) we discussed the observation of PAH molecules in the bipolar outflow of the starburst galaxy M82 armus07, galliano08 . PAHs have been detected far outside the galactic plane at least up to 6 kpc from the disk engel06 , in a region which is spatially correlated with an extended X-ray emission originating from tenuous hot gas (electron density n_ e = 0.013 cm ^ 3 , temperature T = 5.8 10 ^6 K). Our study has demonstrated that, under these conditions, PAHs are destroyed by collisions with thermal electrons on a timescale of few thousand years. Our conclusion was that the PAH survival is possible only if the molecules are isolated from this hot gas, probably in cooler cloudlets of PDR-type gas entrained in the galactic wind.

sources.summary: The paper classifies M82 as HC3N-rich but repeatedly cautions that its nearby, extended emission probably makes its HC3N/HCN ratios too high in several comparisons.

quotes.quote: The galaxies in our survey that do not fulfil the criterion _ s _ mb are IC 342 , M82 , Maffei 2 , and NGC 253 , and we therefore expect the real HC _3 N/HCN and HC _3 N/HNC ratios to be somewhat lower for these galaxies. The galaxies thus seen as HC _3 N-luminous or HC _3 N-rich are NGC 4418 , IC 342 , Circinus , M82 , Maffei 2 , Arp 220 , and IRAS 17208-0014 . We note that two of the three nearby galaxies in the sample, M82 and IC 342 , are showing too high HC _3 N/HCN ratios to fit into the pattern of the figure, which was expected (see Appendix app:nearby2 ). The non-detections of OH megamasers and OH absorbers reported in darling07 are also listed, most of them being either HC _3 N-poor or without any HC _3 N detection (the exceptions are IC 342 and M82 , whose HC _3 N/HCN ratios probably are overestimated, see Appendix app:nearby2 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes M 82 as the prototypical starburst, using hydronium observations and high-PDR modeling as an extragalactic comparison for enhanced cosmic-ray chemistry.

quotes.quote: In Baye09a we selected well-known galaxies, such as Arp 220 or M 82 as examples of sources with active nuclei, and where therefore the cosmic ray ionization rate may be enhanced. Arp 220 is the prototypical ultraluminous galaxy while M82 is the prototypical starburst. Recently VanderTak08 H _ 3 O ^+ has been discovered in these two regions and its fractional abundance has been estimated to be 2--10 10 ^ 9 . These authors found that observations of M 82 are matched by a high- PDR, i.e., an evolved starburst while X-ray models are best at reproducing the observations of Arp 220.

sources.summary: The paper maps M82 in CO isotopologues, finding widespread CO emission, tentative 13CO detections, nuclear C18O emission, unusually high line ratios, and a lower molecular-gas conversion factor.

quotes.quote: For M82, the starburst galaxy, \ emission was detected at all 47 positions that were mapped in the central 4' 2.5' region, while ^ 13 CO was tentatively detected at 15 positions. C ^ 18 O emission was only detected at 13 points close to the nuclear regions in M51 and M82.
The average \ in the center and disk of the galaxies are 9.9 3.0 and 5.6 1.9 respectively, much lower than the peculiar ( 24) found in the center of M82.
The H _2 column density derived from C ^ 18 O shows that the Galactic standard conversion factor ( X factor) overestimates the amount of the molecular gas in M82 by a factor of 2.5.

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as an archetypal nearby starburst with constrained hot-wind parameters, measured diffuse X-ray luminosities, infrared luminosity, and inferred diffuse fractions.

quotes.quote: Recently, constrained the wind parameters in the archetypal nearby starburst galaxy M82 using hard X-ray observations of its central region, finding a high thermalization efficiency ( 1 ) and a mass-loading efficiency of M _ hot / SFR 0.5 .

The observed extended X-ray luminosity in M82 from 0.1 to 2.4 keV band is L_ X ^ 0.1-2.4 keV 1.9 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 ( Strickland97 ), and in the 2-8 keV band is L_ X ^ 2-8 keV 4.4 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 ( SH07 ). The total 8-1000 m infrared luminosity of M82 L_ IR 5.6 10^ 10 L_ ( Sanders03 ) corresponds to a SFR of 5-10 M_ yr ^ 1 ( OConnell78 ; Kennicutt98 ; FS03 ; Strickland04 ; Elbaz07 ; SH09 ; Panuzzo10 ), depending on the assumed IMF. If we convert the observed diffuse X-ray from 2-8 keV to 0.5-8 keV with a conversion factor of 1.5, as assumed in , we get the diffuse fraction f_ d 0.16 for the upper bound of SFR as 10 M_ yr ^ 1 , and f_ d 0.32 for a lower estimated SFR =5 M_ yr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents sensitive GBT HCN and HCO+ maps of M82, finding dense gas tied to star formation, a rotating torus, outflow-associated emission, and enhanced starburst-region efficiency.

quotes.quote: Here we demonstrate the power of the Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope -- the largest single-dish millimeter radio telescope -- for mapping dense gas in galaxies by presenting the most sensitive maps yet of \ and \ in the starburst galaxy M82. The \ and \ in the disk of this galaxy correlates with both recent star formation and more diffuse molecular gas and shows kinematics consistent with a rotating torus. The \ emission extending to the north and south of the disk is coincident with the outflow previously identified in CO and traces the eastern edge of the hot outflowing gas.
The central starburst region has a higher ratio of star formation to dense gas than the outer regions, pointing to the starburst as a key driver of this relationship.

sources.summary: M 82 is used as an archetypical starburst comparison galaxy whose mid-J CO behavior appears normal and whose high-J CO SLED resembles Mrk 231 under similar color conditions.

quotes.quote: Three are from the references listed above: M 82, an archetypical starburst; NGC 1266, a disk galaxy with possible AGN-driven molecular outflows (Alatalo et al. 2011); and NGC 1068, a well-known Seyfert galaxy with its CO line emission detected up to J = 30 (Hailey-Dunsheath et al. 2012) and f_ AGN 50 (Telesco Decher 1988). M 82 is slightly extended and its L_ IR used here was limited to within the same beam size as for the CO SLED.
Indeed, all the other superwind galaxies (e.g., Arp 220 and M 82) show a ``normal'' R_ midCO .

Furthermore, the model prediction that the XDR-associated CO emission occurs mainly at higher J levels is also supported by the observation that, with comparable C(60/100) , M 82 and Mrk 231 have almost identical CO SLEDs for J 10 (Pereira-Santaella et al. 2013).

sources.summary: The paper connects M82 to SN 2014J polarization, describing its dust lane, edge-on starburst disk, multiple absorbing clouds, and small-grain ISM model.

quotes.quote: In particular, it is fully consistent with the position angle distribution along the dust lane of M82 (Jones ; see his Figures 4 and 5) and identified as being generated by transmission (as opposed to scattering).

The observed behaviour results from the combined presence of numerous clouds along the line-of-sight, spanning a wide range of velocities (and most probably positions) within the host. For this reason, we conclude that what is observed in SN 2014J reflects the average properties of the disk of M82, which is seen almost edge-on.

In this respect, to explain the extinction curve of NGC 3034 (M82), Hutton et al. ( ) suggest that small grains are entrained in the supernovae-driven wind region and would reflect the general properties of the galaxy ISM.

sources.summary: The paper argues that M82, as a nearby star-burst galaxy, is the likely source of the Northern TA Hot Spot, with UHECR light nuclei coherently bent by magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: The Ursa Major Cluster or Virgo are too far, but a closer source such as the M82 star-burst galaxy could survive the distance. The so-called TA Hot Spot (about a dozen events) is not centered on M82, but is coherently bent by ( 15-20^ ) in nearby Northern Galactic fields (see fig. fig-2 fig-5 and fig. fig-magnetic fig-magnetic2 ). However, we recognize in the same bending tail a much nearer, narrower and very recent (this year) a 5 event clustering, confirming such a probable coherent bending of UHECR light nuclei from M82. Such a quintuplet cluster within 100 square degrees has a chance probability of <10^ 3 to occur.

sources.summary: The paper uses M82 as the archetypal strong starburst wind system: prior wind simulations match it, the CC model was proposed for it, and its wind has high terminal speeds.

quotes.quote: Simulations of thermally driven winds ( , , ), radiation driven winds ( , ), and cosmic ray driven winds ( , , , , ) have been developed and successfully reproduce observations of galaxies, including the Milky Way and the well-known starburst galaxy, M82.

This model was originally proposed to describe M82, a well-studied starburst galaxy with an extremely strong wind. X-ray fits indicate high terminal wind speeds of up to 1400-2200 km/s ( ), so far above the escape velocity of the galaxy that there is no need to include gravity in a model to describe M82; hence, the CC model omits it.
In M82, f_ d 0.1 ( ); however, many of the galaxies included in Mineo's sample are not M82-like.

sources.summary: The paper presents M82 as a nearby irregular starburst galaxy, brighter than the Milky Way, interacting with M81, and having a high CCSN rate including SN2008iz.

quotes.quote: The M82 galaxy, five times brighter than the Milky Way, exhibits starburst behavior incited by gravitational interaction with M81, a neighboring galaxy . Located at RA 9^ h 55^ m 52.7^ s and Dec 69^ 40^ ' 46^ " , M82 is an irregular starburst galaxy at a distance 3.52 Mpc from Earth . Its CCSN rate is estimated to be (2.1-20) 10^ 2 CCSNe yr ^ 1 . The most recent CCSN in M82 was SN2008iz, a Type II SN first observed on May 3, 2008 .

sources.summary: The paper treats M82 as a nuclear starburst outlier whose saturated WISE core, compact high-SFR disk, and early-stage confined outflow affect its coronal X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The above procedure works for all our sample galaxies, except for M82, the WISE W4 intensity of which is saturated in the central region. We thus replace the SFR of the galaxy with that estimated from the far-IR luminosity as obtained in Paper I.

Indeed, our sample in general shows little correlation between R_ W4 /D_ 25 and SFR/M_* , except for the two outstanding nuclear starburst galaxies, M82 and NGC5253, each of which has the SFR about an order of magnitude greater than the sample average and is a factor of a few more compact.

For example, the major outflow from the galactic nuclear region of M82 (Galaxy 2) is apparently still at an early stage; coronal gas is largely confined and is undergoing strong mixing with surrounding cool gas e.g., Zhang14 , which is at least partially responsible for the relatively large specific X-ray luminosity of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats M82 as a rejected Planck blackbox candidate: a nearby infrared-bright starburst whose microwave emission comes from synchrotron, free-free, and dust components and shows positive spectral curvature.

quotes.quote: Three of the sources (NGC 253, NGC 891, and M82) are nearby infrared-bright starburst galaxies Sanders03 .

The 100 GHz luminosity of these galaxies is a combination of synchrotron emission from cosmic ray electrons, free-free emission from H II regions, and thermal emission from dust Williams10 .
